content:
Let \(R=9\) be the radius of \(C_2\), and write \(OP=x\).  Call the center of \(C_1\) point \(O_1\) and its radius \(r\).  Since \(C_1\) is tangent internally to \(C_2\) at \(A\), we have 
\[
OO_1=R-r=9-r,
\]
and the right‐angle condition from “tangent at \(P\) passes through \(O\)” gives
\[
OO_1^2 \;=\; OP^2+O_1P^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(9-r)^2 \;=\; x^2+r^2.
\]
Hence 
\[
x^2=(9-r)^2-r^2=81-18r.
\]
Also, the ray \(OP\) meets \(C_2\) again at \(Q\), so \(\,OQ=9\) and 
\[
PQ=9-x.
\]
The tangent at \(A\) to \(C_1\) is perpendicular to \(OO_1\) (horizontal), hence is the vertical line \(x=9\).  Its intersection \(R\) with the line \(OP\) lies at distance
\[
OR=\frac{9}{\cos\theta},
\]
where \(\theta=\angle P O x\text{-axis},\) so
\[
QR=OR-OQ=\frac{9}{\cos\theta}-9.
\]
Requiring \(PQ=QR\) gives
\[
9-x=\frac{9}{\cos\theta}-9
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\cos\theta=\frac{9}{18-x}.
\]
On the other hand one finds from the right‐triangle condition that
\[
\cos\theta=\frac{OP_x}{OP}
=\frac{18x}{81+x^2}.
\]
Equating the two expressions for \(\cos\theta\) leads to
\[
\frac{18x}{81+x^2}=\frac{9}{18-x}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x^2-12x+27=0
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x=3\quad(\text{reject }x=9\text{ as degenerate}).
\]
Hence 
\[
\boxed{OP=3}.
\]
